text: The surgeon visually inspects the oblique fissure, with the lower lobe positioned at the bottom of the field. The middle lobe is being lifted to enhance exposure.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the pulmonary artery, navigating a relatively good fissure while being cautious of visible lymph nodes and fibrous tissue identified in the PET scan.
The surgeon navigates to the posterior oblique fissure, where the light from the bronchoscope is visible inside the trachea, illuminating the area behind the fissure.

The surgeon divides the posterior oblique fissure, revealing the right main bronchus beneath the azygos vein. The bronchus is observed to bifurcate into the upper lobe bronchus and the bronchus intermedius.

The surgeon dissects a prominent vein descending to the lower lobe, noting its unusual anatomical course as it typically originates from the posterior segment of the upper lobe and extends to the apex of the lower lobe.

The surgeon divides the horizontal fissure and excises R-eleven lymph nodes located beneath an aberrant pulmonary vein. The dissection continues on the pulmonary artery to identify the middle lobe branches and remove another R-eleven lymph node.
The surgeon carefully lifts the aberrant pulmonary vein to expose the posterior ascending arterial branch of the pulmonary artery.
The surgeon dissects the inferior pulmonary ligament and removes several R-nine lymph nodes, located in the lower region of the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon dissects the pleura posterior to the inferior pulmonary ligament, following along the bronchus intermedius.
The surgeon dissects between the right upper lobe bronchus and the bronchus intermedius, encountering an abnormal vein with unclear anatomy. A small branch of this vein is cauterized.

The surgeon continues dissecting the R-eleven lymph node located between the right upper lobe bronchus and the bronchus intermedius, revealing the arterial and venous anatomy. The pleura is opened posterior to the superior vena cava and over the azygous vein as the dissection progresses towards the two R and four R regions for lymphadenectomy.
The surgeon removes a large four R lymph node packet using a glove finger technique. The view then shifts back into the pulmonary fissure for further examination.

The surgeon identifies an aberrant vein originating from the posterior segment of the right upper lobe vein, extending towards the apex of the lower lobe. Behind this vein, a posterior ascending branch to the upper lobe is visible, which the surgeon aims to preserve. The aberrant vessel is stapled, confirming its pathway to the lower lobe.

The posterior ascending branch of the pulmonary artery is clearly visible. The surgeon continues to dissect R-XI lymph nodes adjacent to the upper lobe bronchus and some R-XII lymph nodes in the upper lobe, noting the N-I positivity in this area from the scan.
The surgeon dissects R-X lymph nodes at the front while identifying the middle lobe vein. The middle lobe vein is then divided using a surgical stapler.
A staple misfire is observed as the blade fails to reach the end of the tissue. The surgeon uses forceps to grasp the remaining branch and successfully divides it with a second staple firing.
The surgeon uses a robotic arm to maneuver the stapler around the lower lobe vein, successfully dividing the vein.
The surgeon returns to the fissure, where the pulmonary artery is visible. With the middle lobe vein already divided, the surgeon uses a stapler to complete the division of the horizontal fissure.
The surgeon divides the lower lobe and middle lobe branches of the pulmonary artery using two staple firings, ensuring preservation of the posterior ascending branch to the upper lobe.

The surgeon staples the bronchus intermedius just below the right upper lobe bronchus, using bronchoscopic vision to ensure the inclusion of the right upper lobe takeoff. The lower and middle lobes are removed en bloc as a single specimen due to the presence of N-Y lymph nodes.
The surgeon places the bilobe specimen into a plastic bag and extracts it through a low anterior assistant port.
The surgeon meticulously checks the surgical site to ensure all lymph nodes are removed, guided by previous PET scan findings.
